title: HARBOR: Automated Harness Optimization
abstract: Long-horizon language-model agents are dominated, in lines of code and in operational complexity, not by their underlying model but by the harness that wraps it: context compaction, tool caching, semantic memory, trajectory reuse, speculative tool prediction, and the glue that binds the model to a sandboxed execution environment. We argue that harness design is a first-class machine-learning problem and that automated configuration search dominates manual stacking once the flag space exceeds a handful of bits. We formalize automated harness optimization as constrained noisy Bayesian optimization over a mixed-variable, cost-heterogeneous configuration space with cold-start-corrected rewards and a posterior chance-constrained safety check, and give a reference solver, HARBOR, built from a block-additive SAAS surrogate, multi-fidelity cost-aware acquisition, and TuRBO trust regions. We instantiate the problem in a flag-gated harness over a production coding agent and compare controlled manual tuning with an end-to-end HARBOR run.

title: MAS-FIRE: Fault Injection and Reliability Evaluation for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems
abstract: As LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed for complex tasks, ensuring their reliability has become a pressing challenge. Since these systems coordinate through unstructured natural language, they are prone to semantic failures that propagate silently. We propose MAS-FIRE, a systematic framework for fault injection and reliability evaluation. It defines 15 fault types covering intra-agent cognitive errors and inter-agent coordination failures and injects them through prompt modification, response rewriting, and message-routing manipulation. Applied to three representative architectures, MAS-FIRE reveals a tiered set of fault-tolerant behaviors and shows that stronger foundation models do not uniformly improve robustness, while iterative closed-loop designs neutralize over 40 percent of faults that cause catastrophic collapse in linear workflows.

title: Claw-Eval: Towards Trustworthy Evaluation of Autonomous Agents
abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for multi-step workflows in real-world software environments, but existing benchmarks are limited by trajectory-opaque grading, underspecified safety and robustness evaluation, and narrow coverage. We introduce Claw-Eval, an end-to-end suite with 300 human-verified tasks across general service orchestration, multimodal interaction, and multi-turn professional dialogue. Each run is recorded through execution traces, audit logs, and environment snapshots, yielding 2,159 rubric items. The protocol evaluates completion, safety, and robustness across three trials. Experiments on 14 frontier models show that trajectory-opaque evaluation misses many safety and robustness failures, capability does not imply consistency, and model rankings vary across task groups and metrics.

title: Game-theoretic statistics and safe anytime-valid inference
abstract: Safe anytime-valid inference provides measures of statistical evidence and certainty, including e-processes for testing and confidence sequences for estimation, that remain valid at all stopping times and accommodate continuous monitoring and optional stopping or continuation. These measures rely on test martingales, understood as wealth processes in betting games. The paper summarizes the goals and philosophy of safe anytime-valid inference and reviews advances in testing composite hypotheses and estimating functionals in nonparametric settings.

title: Performative Prediction
abstract: When predictions support decisions they may influence the outcomes they aim to predict. This work develops a risk-minimization framework for performative prediction that combines statistics, game theory, and causality. It introduces performative stability, under which predictions are calibrated against future outcomes that arise from acting on the prediction, and gives necessary and sufficient conditions for retraining to converge to a performatively stable point of nearly minimal loss.

title: The stratified micro-randomized trial design: sample size considerations for testing nested causal effects of time-varying treatments
abstract: This work develops a stratified micro-randomized trial for settings in which treatment randomization occurs at times determined by predictions constructed from outcomes of prior treatment and where the outcome accrues over a period that may include subsequent treatments. It defines conditional and marginal proximal treatment effects and develops a primary analysis method and sample-size formulas for testing these effects.
